eli3: The Mann-Whitney U test is the non-parametric alternative to the two-sample t-test. Instead of comparing means, it asks: if you pick one observation from group 1 and one from group 2 at random, how often is the group-1 value larger? This probability is P(X₁ > X₂) — the test checks whether this is 0.5 (no difference) or not.

The test works by combining both samples, ranking all values from 1 to n₁+n₂, then computing the sum of ranks in each group. If both groups have the same distribution, the rank sums should be approximately equal. If one group has consistently higher values, its rank sum will be larger.

Because it uses ranks rather than raw values, Mann-Whitney makes no normality assumption. It is robust to outliers, works with ordinal data, and is valid for any continuous distribution. The cost is slightly lower power than the t-test when the normality assumption actually holds.

whenWhy.title: Non-normal data or small samples
whenWhy.body: When the normality assumption is untenable (severely skewed data, ordinal outcomes, small n), Mann-Whitney is the appropriate alternative to the two-sample t-test. It is exact for any distribution and any n — no large-sample approximation needed (though normal approximation works well for n₁,n₂ ≥ 10).

whenWhy.title: Ordinal outcomes
whenWhy.body: Pain scales (0–10), Likert scales (1–5), severity ratings, and education levels are ordinal — the intervals between ranks are not equal. Computing means of ordinal data is questionable; comparing rank distributions is not. Mann-Whitney is the standard test for ordinal outcomes in medical and social research.

whenWhy.title: Comparing medians or stochastic dominance
whenWhy.body: Mann-Whitney tests the null H₀: P(X₁ > X₂) = 0.5, which is equivalent to testing equal distributions (not just equal medians — a common misconception). The test statistic U/n₁n₂ estimates P(X₁ > X₂), which is the AUC of a ROC curve comparing the two distributions.

whenWhy.title: Robust to outliers
whenWhy.body: Because ranks are used, extreme outliers move at most one rank position (from being the largest to being even larger). The t-test statistic is heavily affected by one extreme outlier inflating s; Mann-Whitney's U statistic is not. This makes it preferable for data with heavy tails or suspected measurement errors.

deepDiveIntro: The Mann-Whitney statistic counts how many times a value from group 1 exceeds a value from group 2 across all possible pairings. Under the null hypothesis that both groups have the same distribution, this count should be close to half the total number of pairs. The equivalent Wilcoxon rank-sum statistic works by ranking all observations together and summing the ranks from one group.

For samples of at least 10 in each group, the statistic is approximately normally distributed, and a standard z-score can be used to compute the p-value. For smaller samples, exact critical values are available in tables. Tied values are handled by assigning mid-ranks and applying a small correction to the variance.

deepDive.heading: Test Statistic U
deepDive.body: The Mann-Whitney statistic compares the two groups through pairwise ordering rather than raw numerical distances. It can be interpreted as counting how often an observation from one group exceeds an observation from the other. That is why the test is naturally connected to ranks, stochastic dominance, and discrimination metrics such as AUC.

deepDive.heading: Large-Sample Normal Approximation
deepDive.body: For larger samples, the rank-based statistic can be converted into an approximately normal score, which makes p-value computation straightforward. Exact calculations are still possible in small samples, but the normal approximation is usually adequate once both groups are moderately sized. Ties require a variance correction because repeated values reduce the amount of ranking information available.

deepDive.heading: U as AUC and Effect Size
deepDive.body: A useful interpretation of the test is probabilistic: it estimates how often a randomly chosen value from one group exceeds a randomly chosen value from the other. That same quantity is exactly the area under the ROC curve when the variable is used for classification. This makes the test easy to connect with effect-size language and ranking performance.

deepDive.heading: Assumptions and What the Test Actually Tests
deepDive.body: The Mann-Whitney test is often described as a test of medians, but that description is only safe in special cases. More generally, it compares the two full distributions through their rank ordering, so it can react to differences in spread or shape as well as location. That broader interpretation is important when communicating what a significant result really means.

takeaways: U₁ = #{pairs where X > Y}; under H₀: E[U₁] = n₁n₂/2; equivalent to Wilcoxon rank-sum test. [1]
U₁/(n₁n₂) = P̂(X>Y) = AUC — links non-parametric testing directly to classifier evaluation. [1][2]
Large-sample approximation: z = (U₁−n₁n₂/2)/√(n₁n₂(n₁+n₂+1)/12) ~ N(0,1) for n₁,n₂ ≥ 10. [1]
No normality assumption; valid for ordinal data and any continuous distribution. [1]
Lower power than t-test when normality holds (ARE = 3/π ≈ 0.955 for normal data). [1]
Tests H₀: F₁=F₂, not H₀: median₁=median₂ — a common misconception. [1]

edgeCases.q: What is the ARE (asymptotic relative efficiency) of Mann-Whitney vs t-test?
edgeCases.a: For normally distributed data: ARE = 3/π ≈ 0.955. This means Mann-Whitney needs about 1/0.955 ≈ 1.05× as many observations as the t-test to achieve the same power — almost identical efficiency. For heavier-tailed distributions (e.g. Laplace, t with few df), the ARE exceeds 1 — Mann-Whitney is MORE efficient than the t-test. This makes it a nearly free robustness improvement. [1]

edgeCases.q: How do ties affect the Mann-Whitney test?
edgeCases.a: Ties between observations in the same group don't matter (they don't affect between-group comparisons). Ties between groups are assigned mid-ranks (average of tied positions). The variance formula must be corrected for ties. Many implementations handle this automatically. If >25% of observations are tied, consider the sign test or a permutation test instead. [1]

sources.label: Casella & Berger — Statistical Inference (2nd ed.), Ch. 10
Hollander, Wolfe & Chicken — Nonparametric Statistical Methods (3rd ed.), Ch. 4
